topic_definition: The informational substrate that enables effective engineering work: human- and machine-readable documentation, observability and telemetry, data-pipeline hygiene, product/business literacy, and cross-functional communication artifacts. Captures language about authoring and maintaining the context (docs, runbooks, dashboards, ADRs, data integrations, stakeholder alignment) on which engineering decisions depend, as opposed to the act of writing code itself.

concepts.name: Technical documentation authoring & maintenance
concepts.definition: Writing, updating, and curating engineering-facing docs that describe how systems work and why.

topic_calibration_recommendations: Run the matcher on a stratified sample (~300 postings across 2024 and 2026, mixed seniority) and pull a hit list per concept. (1) Verify that bare-token matches for 'documentation', 'docs', 'data', 'context', 'monitor', 'metrics', 'communication', 'stakeholder', 'RFC' are gated by their compound/guard rules — measure precision before/after the guard. (2) For 'cross-functional' and 'stakeholder' compounds, expect near-100% saturation in senior JDs; consider reporting per-posting *count* (density) rather than binary presence. (3) Decompose role/seniority cells (junior/mid/senior/staff+/EM) before time-trend interpretation: senior+ JDs already mention ADRs, runbooks, and observability heavily, so a temporal shift is meaningful only conditional on level. (4) Spot-check named-tool hits (Datadog, Grafana, dbt, Airflow) against a list of role titles to ensure they are not data-engineer-only artifacts contaminating SWE counts. (5) Track the 'technical writing' concept separately from 'cross-functional communication' — they have different signal profiles and the hypothesis (humans pivot to context curation) predicts the former should rise faster than the latter.
topic_notes: Ordering is intentional: artifact-side concepts (docs, ADRs, runbooks, API docs) are listed before behavior-side concepts (system understanding, product literacy, cross-functional communication, technical writing) so that downstream factor analyses can group them. Observability and data-pipeline concepts are kept here (rather than in a separate 'platform' topic) because the hypothesis is about *informational substrate quality*, not platform engineering per se — telemetry and data hygiene are what make the substrate trustworthy. Deliberately excluded: testing/QA (verification topic), code review (verification/process), CI/CD pipelines (process scaffolding), agile ceremonies (process scaffolding), task management/Jira (orchestration/process), prompt engineering and agent management (orchestration). 'Mentorship' is excluded because it overlaps with leadership and is captured elsewhere; only narrowly scoped knowledge-sharing artifacts (onboarding docs, tech talks, internal blog) are included here.
